mappings.rationaleAI: Element classification by periodic law (A), groups of elements (B), other classifications (C), and origin and abundances in nature (D).
Organization by atomic number expressing periodic law (A), with block structure linking to element groups (B).
Mendeleev's formulation of the periodic law is the foundational contribution to systematic element classification (A).
Lavoisier's identification of oxygen and hydrogen as elements contributed to element classification (A). His characterization of oxygen connects to the oxygen group (B.7).
Hydrogen, its forms, isotopes, compounds, and water are specifically outlined (B.1).
Water's structure, forms, and properties are covered under hydrogen's compounds (B.1).
Nitrogen as a Group Va element covering occurrence, properties, and compounds (B.6).
Oxygen as a Group VIa element covering occurrence, history, and properties (B.7).
Carbon as a Group IVa element (B.5), and C-14 for dating connects to element origins (D).
Silicon as a Group IVa element (B.5) and second most abundant crustal element (D.1.a).
Iron as a first transition series element (B.11.a) and most abundant element by mass on Earth (D.1.a).
Copper as a first transition series element (B.11.a).
Silver as a second transition series element (B.11.b).
Gold as a third transition series element (B.11.b) and a noble metal (C.4).
Distinction between stable and radioactive elements (C.2).
Atomic structure underlies the systematic classification of elements (A).
Stellar nucleosynthesis connects to element origins in stars (D.3).
Solar composition contributes to element abundances in the solar system (D.2).
Atmospheric composition relates to element occurrence in the atmosphere (D.1.c).
Inorganic chemistry spans element groups (B) and their classifications (C).
Organic chemistry's centrality of carbon connects to the carbon group elements (B.5).
Mineral composition connects to element occurrence in the crust (D.1.a).
Earth's crust, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere are domains for element occurrence (D.1).
Alloys of transition metals connect to transition element properties and uses (B.11).
Classification of metals, semimetals, and nonmetals (C.1) and technologically significant elements (C.7).
CO2 as an atmospheric trace gas connects to element occurrence in the atmosphere (D.1.c).
Geological science connects to element occurrence in the crust (D.1.a).
Sodium chloride connects to the alkali metals group (B.2).
Ocean salinity connects to element occurrence in the hydrosphere (D.1.b).
Photosynthesis concentrates elements in the biosphere (D.1.d), biologically active elements (C.6).
Venus's atmosphere provides data on element abundances in the solar system (D.2).
Soil mineral composition connects to element occurrence in the crust (D.1.a).
